Clinical information: A 57-year-old female patient complained of pain and discomfort on the left side of the face for four years. Ocular assessment showed no abnormalities, and vision was intact.Intraorally, ulcerated gingival mucosa was noted. The affected areas exhibited pain, bleeding on provocation, severe clinical attachment loss, and root surface denudation. Severe halitosis was also reported. Oral hygiene in the affected sites was poor, with the presence of soft debris and calculus deposits.Complete blood picture showed values within normal limits except for eosinophil percentage, which was increased to 8%. Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) investigation was nonreactive for HIV-1 and HIV-2 (Ag/Ab) using the CMIA method, indicating negative status. Thyroid and liver function tests were within normal ranges. Blood coagulation parameters and the diabetes profile likewise fell within the normal reference range.With the above data, a provisional diagnosis of necrotizing ulcerative periodontitis (NUP) was made, and the patient was subjected to scaling and root surface debridement. Copious irrigation with betadine saline solution was carried out. The patient was advised with proper oral hygiene instructions and antibiotics for 5 days, along with topical application of Metrogyl DG Gel thrice daily and 3% hydrogen peroxide mouthwash with 1:3 dilution twice daily.However,there was no discernible improvement at the next appointment; instead, the clinical symptoms worsened much more.

Clinical information: A previously healthy 20-year-old woman presented with a 10-month history of a persistent cough and progressive exertional dyspnea. High-resolution computed tomography (HRCT) of the chest revealed diffuse bronchiectasis, patchy ground-glass opacities, and pulmonary hyperinflation. However, despite repeated treatments with intravenous corticosteroids, inhaled bronchodilators and antibiotics, her symptoms worsened, and her physical tolerance significantly deteriorated. One month prior to admission, she experienced a spontaneous pneumomediastinum and subcutaneous emphysema. Additionally, she had been suffering from persistent and painful oral mucosal erosions for the past 18 months, which had severely affected her appetite and led to a weight loss of 15 kg. She had no rashes, abdominal pain, fever, or night sweats. She denied smoking or exposure to toxic gasses, and her family history was unremarkable.On admission, her vitals were normal, and pulse oxygen saturation of 98% on room air. Multiple oral mucosal erosions were observed. No conjunctival or anogenital lesions were noted. She had no skin lesions. On pulmonary auscultation, wheezes were identified at the end of expiration bilaterally. Cardiac examination showed regular heart rhythm without any murmurs. Her abdomen was tender and painless, and no definitive mass was found on palpation. She had no peripheral enlarged lymph nodes or edema.
Laboratory investigations, including a complete blood count, metabolic profile, and inflammatory biomarkers, were within normal limits. The serum aspergillus-specific IgE tests were negative, which did not support the diagnosis of allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis.
Considering clinical diagnosis of constrictive bronchiolitis, a contrast-enhanced CT scan was conducted to screen underlying tumors. A pelvic mass exhibiting pronounced enhancement and hypertrophied vessels was observed. Positron emission tomography (PET) scan revealed increased metabolic activity in the pelvic mass, along with multiple hypermetabolic lymph nodes adjacent to the bilateral iliac vessels. An ultrasound-guided percutaneous biopsy of the pelvic tumor was performed, which indicated a pathological diagnosis of Castleman’s disease.

Clinical information: A 36-year-old Asian female presented with oral lesion. She had type II diabetes mellitus and asthma. Family history of asthma was reported. The patient was diagnosed with lichen planus pemphigoides 10 months ago and was started on oral prednisolone and azathioprine with minimal improvement. A weight gain of 15 Kg over 6 months was noted secondary to steroid therapy. She also complained of shortness of breath for which a chest CT scan was carried out. CT scan findings included bronchial dilation, air trapping and bronchial wall thickening. An incidental finding of a left sided retroperitoneal mass was also made. This was further investigated by a CT abdomen/pelvis and on account of suspicion of neuroendocrine tumor, a PET/CT Ga-68 Dotatate scan was performed as well. This revealed an avid left retroperitoneal soft tissue density mass with a maximum dimension of 6.7 cm.On physical examination, oral mucosal and skin lesions on hands, abdomen and back were noted. Skin lesions present as macules.Her respiratory examination revealed ronchi and a few crackles at the base of the right lung. Shortness of breath on exertion was noted as well. Laboratory investigations revealed normal serum albumin, hemoglobin, WBC and platelet count. Pulmonary function tests (PFTs) showed a severe obstructive pattern without reversibility, thereby confirming the diagnosis of bronchiolitis obliterans. Histopathology of the retroperitoneal mass mass confirmed the diagnosis of the hyaline vascular type of Castleman disease.
